messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Speech recognition for low-resource languages or specialized
domains lacks transcribed audio; how can we leverage synthetic
data or transfer learning to reach competitive accuracy with
minimal real labeled data?


### Proposed method
1) Build the right text and language model first. Retrieve and transform in-domain text using a dataset-retrieval-and-transformation pipeline: mine public corpora, manuals, chat logs, and domain documents that match the target language/domain; then normalize to ASR style (spoken-form punctuation, numeral expansion, abbreviations, code-switch variants) and expand with rare terms and morphology. From this script, construct a pronunciation lexicon or grapheme mapping and train a compact domain language model (e.g., 4–6 gram and a small transformer LM) for shallow fusion or WFST decoding. Deduplicate and stratify the script for coverage over word frequency bins, numerics, entities, and prosody markers.

2) Turn the script into strong synthetic audio with transfer-learned multilingual TTS. Start from a robust multilingual TTS trained on high-resource languages; fine-tune lightly toward the target language or accent using any available transcribed minutes or pseudo-transcripts, leveraging codec-based acoustic tokenization to preserve cross-lingual prosody and phonotactics. Synthesize 100–1000 hours with multiple speakers, speaking rates, and style/noise profiles; augment on-the-fly with room impulse responses, codecs, and gain perturbations. Ensure phoneme and syllable coverage by sampling the script with a curriculum that upweights rare words and domain terms early and ramps toward natural frequency later.

3) Pretrain the ASR encoder with a unified supervised plus self-supervised objective. Use a wav2vec 2.0–style stack with a quantizer and transformer context network. Jointly optimize (i) CTC on high-resource labeled speech and on a quality-controlled subset of the synthetic speech-text pairs to encourage phone-aligned features, and (ii) masked contrastive learning on large amounts of unlabeled target audio mixed with the remaining synthetic audio to adapt the codebook and contextual space to the target acoustics and vocabulary. After convergence, freeze the feature encoder or apply a low learning rate to stabilize downstream tuning.

4) Fine-tune with minimal real labels, strong pseudo-labels, and a calibrated curriculum. Use a strong multilingual teacher ASR to transcribe the unlabeled target audio; keep only high-confidence segments and filter with the domain lexicon and LM to reduce homophone and OOV errors. Train the student on a mixture of the small real labeled set, high-confidence pseudo-labels, and a downweighted sample of synthetic pairs. Start with real+pseudo data, anneal synthetic weight as WER drops, and gradually admit lower-confidence pseudo-labels if the dev set improves. Use CTC or RNN-T loss with SpecAugment and speed perturbation; add parameter-efficient adapters or selectively unfreeze upper transformer layers for domain adaptation. Decode with beam search and shallow-fuse the domain LM; optionally rescore with the transformer LM. Iterate: re-transcribe unlabeled audio with the improved student, refresh the LM on new transcripts, and regenerate a small tranche of harder synthetic utterances focused on remaining error modes until dev WER plateaus.

### Rationale
Domain-matched text determines the vocabulary and n-gram statistics that drive ASR decoding; retrieving and transforming existing datasets yields high-coverage scripts without costly manual authoring. High-fidelity multilingual TTS, trained via transfer, can then turn these scripts into acoustically diverse, label-perfect pairs that inject the right terms, numbers, and entities into the acoustic model and LM, even when human transcripts are scarce.

Unified supervised plus self-supervised pretraining aligns the encoder to phonetic structure while adapting its latent space to target acoustics using abundant unlabeled audio. This produces features that transfer with little labeled data. Finally, confidence-filtered pseudo-labeling exploits all available target audio to close the domain gap, while curriculum weighting and parameter-efficient adaptation prevent overfitting to synthetic artifacts. Shallow-fusion with the domain LM ensures the decoder respects target phrasing and terminology, pushing accuracy toward competitive levels with only a small real labeled set.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] A Thorough Examination of Decoding Methods in the Era of LLMs (2402.06925)
   problem: Practitioners lack clear, LLM-era guidance for selecting decoding methods across diverse tasks and alignment settings; many prior conclusions were drawn on task-specific models and may not transfer.
   method:  Conduct a comprehensive benchmark of deterministic (greedy, beam search, diverse beam search, contrastive search, contrastive decoding, FSD/FSD-d, DoLa) and stochastic (temperature, top-p, top-k, η, mirostat, typical) decoding across coding, math, summarization, translation, commonsense, factuality, instruction-following, and open-ended generation on unaligned/aligned Llama-2-7B and other models. Findings: deterministic methods generally outperform stochastic ones on closed-ended tasks, while stochastic methods excel in open-ended generation (e.g., greedy/beam/DBS underperform on MAUVE) (lines 127–131, 114). Alignment narrows performance gaps and reduces dependence on decoding (RDP drops; entropy lower for aligned models, Table 2) (lines 133–138, 141–147). Deterministic methods reduce hallucinations and improve instruction following (higher FActScore and AlpacaEval vs stochastic) (line 139). Among stochastic methods, low-temperature sampling is typically best on closed-ended tasks (line 148). Efficiency analysis shows FSD/FSD-d and stochastic methods run near greedy speed, while contrastive search is slowest and contrastive decoding adds a constant overhead; beam/DBS incur moderate, length-growing latency (lines 154–166).

2. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Transformer self-attention in vision foundation models fails to highlight domain-specific spatial relations under large distribution shifts to specialized imagery, limiting transfer.
   method:  Learn a small set of global attention maps shared across images and tokens (e.g., C=10) that encode reusable domain-specific patterns (e.g., horizontal/vertical context for circuits, local neighborhoods for maps). For each token at location (i, j), crop an aligned h×h sub-map from each global map (top-left at (h−i, h−j)), normalize to form token-to-token attention, apply it to values V, and mix the result with the original multi-head attention via learned weights r and balance α, inserting this module in a single mid transformer layer (e.g., 16th). This adds minimal parameters, reduces overfitting, and consistently improves over parameter-efficient transfer baselines like LoRA/AdaptFormer on low-resource tasks.

3. [MULTIMODAL] OFA: Unifying Architectures, Tasks, and Modalities through a Simple Sequence-to-Sequence Learning Framework (2202.03052)
   problem: Multimodal and language models typically rely on task-specific heads/adapters and heterogeneous task formulations, and often entangle visual representations with downstream detectors, hindering zero-shot transfer and true unification across tasks and modalities.
   method:  OFA casts all uni- and cross-modal tasks as instruction-driven sequence-to-sequence generation with a single encoder–decoder Transformer and no task- or modality-specific layers. It builds a shared multimodal token vocabulary by discretizing images into short code sequences and representing objects as label tokens plus discretized bounding-box coordinates, while encoding images via ResNet patch features. Handcrafted instructions unify task interfaces across pretraining, finetuning, and inference, enabling a task- and modality-agnostic I/O and architecture.

4. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [ROBOTICS] RREx-BoT: Remote Referring Expressions with a Bag of Tricks (2301.12614)
   problem: Generic vision–language models are trained on 2D images and lack mechanisms to ground language to objects situated in 3D embodied environments, where spatial relations and local context matter for remote object grounding.
   method:  Adapt a pretrained VL backbone (ViLBERT) to 3D by replacing 2D box encodings with 3D region positional embeddings (x, y, z, r) normalized relative to the observing viewpoint, with depth estimated from the median depth inside each region. Enrich local context by adding Mask-RCNN proposal regions and average features from neighboring viewpoints to each viewpoint batch. Fine-tune with a sigmoid/BCE objective over regions within a sampled viewpoint and apply viewpoint-level negative augmentation (random viewpoints without the target) to suppress false positives. These 3D encodings and context cues significantly improve grounding, as shown by ablations removing 3D encoding and context proposals that reduce RGS/SR (Tables 3–4).

7. [CV] S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging (2412.13156)
   problem: Medical image segmentation models trained on scarce, low-diversity data often learn biased, noisy semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness. Existing fixes (specialized augmentations/architectures) rely on domain assumptions that may not generalize across modalities (CT, MRI, RGB).
   method:  Introduce Semantic Stacking (S2S2), an add-on, domain-agnostic training strategy that estimates a denoised semantic representation by stacking images that share the same ground-truth segmentation map and guiding the network toward that representation. Practically, S2S2 adds a semantic consistency loss between features from two images per mask sampled from the stack and combines it with the standard segmentation loss. The consistency loss is applied to both encoder and decoder features and instantiated as 1 − cosine similarity, improving feature robustness without changing the base architecture or augmentation pipeline. (Paper: Sec. 1, 3; Fig. 2; lines 29–31, 55–74, 89–95, 123–126; Code: TransUNet trainer.py utils.ConsistencyLoss)

8. [CV] Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (2107.11787)
   problem: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation trained with only image-level labels relies on sparse CAM seeds and off-the-shelf saliency maps that are used offline and not adapted to the target data, yielding low-quality pseudo labels and coarse boundaries.
   method:  AuxSegNet is a joint multi-task framework that treats semantic segmentation as the primary task and leverages multi-label image classification and saliency detection as auxiliary tasks, all sharing a backbone and trained end-to-end using only image-level labels and pre-trained saliency maps as initial pseudo labels. The classification branch produces CAMs; the saliency and segmentation branches use atrous conv decoders and are trained with losses on both initial and refined predictions against pseudo labels. Saliency pseudo labels are updated online by fusing the model’s refined saliency with pre-trained saliency through a dense CRF, while segmentation pseudo labels are generated from CAMs (and later their affinity-refined versions), enabling the auxiliary tasks to adapt to the target dataset and improve segmentation performance iteratively.

10. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

11. [RL] Towards Theoretical Understanding of Inverse Reinforcement Learning (2304.12966)
   problem: Estimating the full set of rewards that make an expert policy optimal (the feasible reward set) lacks a principled accuracy metric and stability guarantees with respect to estimation errors in the environment and expert policy.
   method:  Introduce a Hausdorff-distance–based evaluation H_d between feasible reward sets induced by the L∞ metric on rewards (d_G), and define a premetric ρ_G on IRL problems that combines L1 distance between transition models and differences in the support of the expert policy. Prove a Lipschitz continuity result (Theorem 3.2) showing that, with unrestricted bounded rewards, small changes in the IRL problem (per ρ_G) imply small Hausdorff changes in feasible reward sets; and show via counterexamples that common restrictions (state-only, time-homogeneous, β-margin rewards) break this Lipschitz property.

12. [RL] Demonstration-Guided Reinforcement Learning with Efficient Exploration for Task Automation of Surgical Robot (2302.09772)
   problem: Off-policy RL for surgical robot automation struggles with exploration when only a modest set of demonstrations is available. Prior demo-regularized actor-only methods suffer from critic overestimation that assigns high values to expert-unlike actions, hindering efficient exploration.
   method:  Introduce an expert-guided actor-critic framework that regularizes both the critic and the actor using an action-dissimilarity term to expert actions. Concretely, augment the reward with a behavior gap penalty and define a regularized Q-function Qπ(s,a)=E[∑γ^t (rt − γα d(at+1, a^e_{t+1}))], so the critic lowers value estimates for expert-unlike actions while the actor maximizes Q plus a similarity term to expert behavior. This joint regularization mitigates critic overestimation and explicitly steers exploration toward expert-like actions, improving sample efficiency. 13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)

14. [IR_REC] Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval (2509.09459)
   problem: Contrastive fine-tuning of multilingual dense retrievers is hindered by false negatives in hard-negative sets, since top-k retrieved candidates often include relevant but unjudged documents.
   method:  Build a multilingual hard-negative candidate list by fusing representations from multiple retrievers (e.g., mE5, BGE) via a projection-and-concatenation fusion to score candidates, then use an LLM to judge each candidate’s relevance to the query and its positive (0/1/2 for irrelevant/partially/highly relevant) and keep only those judged irrelevant. This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).

15. [IR_REC] Semi-supervised Collaborative Filtering by Text-enhanced Domain Adaptation (2007.07085)
   problem: Cross-domain recommendation on sparse implicit-feedback data without user/item overlap suffers from severe data sparsity and label noise from negative sampling; directly aligning latent embeddings across domains is sub-optimal because different datasets induce different latent spaces.
   method:  Propose TDAR, a text-enhanced domain adaptation framework that uses domain-invariant textual features from reviews as anchors to align source and target. It concatenates fixed textual features with trainable user/item embeddings and feeds them to domain classifiers; embeddings are adversarially trained to confuse the classifiers so user/item representations align across domains in a shared latent-textual space (with separate user/item adaptation nets). On the target domain, training uses only positive labels plus the domain-adaptation signal, abandoning negative sampling to avoid label noise, while the denser source domain supplies negative supervision. This semi-supervised scheme transfers distributional patterns from a dense source to a sparse target and mitigates mislabeling on the target.

16. [SPEECH] UniSpeech: Unified Speech Representation Learning with Labeled and Unlabeled Data (2101.07597)
   problem: Low-resource ASR lacks labeled data, while purely supervised transfer ignores abundant unlabeled target audio and purely self-supervised pretraining may not learn phoneme-aligned, SR-specific features that transfer well across languages and domains.
   method:  Pretrain a wav2vec 2.0-style encoder with a unified multitask objective that combines supervised phonetic CTC and phonetically-aware contrastive self-supervision. On labeled source data, jointly optimize CTC to align contextual representations with phoneme labels and a masked contrastive loss between contextual embeddings and quantized latent targets; on unlabeled target data, apply the same contrastive objective to adapt the codebook and contextual space. The architecture uses a convolutional feature encoder, a Transformer context network, and a Gumbel-softmax vector quantizer with codebook diversity regularization, followed by fine-tuning (freezing the feature encoder) on small labeled target sets.

The mT5-based text conditioning and codec LM enable effective cross-lingual synthesis. Evaluations across seven languages (Spanish, Portuguese, German, Dutch, Hindi, Marathi, Tamil) show consistent MOS improvements over VITS and NaturalSpeech, including large gains in low-resource Indic languages, demonstrating robustness and adaptability of the approach.

18. [LLM_NLP] Better Synthetic Data by Retrieving and Transforming Existing Datasets (2404.14361)
   problem: High-quality, task-specific NLP training data is often scarce. Purely LLM-generated synthetic datasets tend to lack diversity and difficulty, while directly using existing datasets can be misaligned with the target task format and objectives.
   method:  DataTune automatically repurposes public datasets for a new task via two stages: (1) dual-stage dataset retrieval from HuggingFace—first using a bi-encoder retriever (DataFinder) to propose candidates, then an LLM-based reranker that considers dataset descriptions, schema, and sample rows, with self-consistency to select robust datasets; and (2) an LLM-driven transformation pipeline with Task Expansion (enrich task spec), Schema Selection (identify relevant columns), a Planning Module (produce a step-by-step transformation plan), and an Execution Module (apply the plan per row) to generate task-aligned input/output examples. Transforming multiple top-ranked datasets preserves source diversity and yields harder, less-duplicated examples while maintaining label accuracy, leading to consistent fine-tuning gains over few-shot prompting and over using synthetic or retrieved data alone.

19. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.
   method:  Introduce a two-loop framework where an outer loop places a physical fixture to scaffold the task and an inner loop learns the manipulation policy. The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses. The inner loop uses a vision-based RL policy (variant of A3C) from RGB images to end-effector motions executed via operational space control, yielding dramatically faster learning and higher success on peg insertion, wrench manipulation, and shallow-depth insertion.

20. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

21. [ROBOTICS] Beyond Sight: Finetuning Generalist Robot Policies with Heterogeneous Sensors via Language Grounding (2501.04693)
   problem: Generalist robot policies are largely trained on vision and proprioception and lack semantic grounding for scarce but crucial modalities like touch and audio. Naively finetuning on small heterogeneous-sensor datasets causes the policy to ignore new sensors and fails to enable zero-shot multimodal reasoning in partially observable settings.
   method:  FuSe finetunes pre-trained generalist policies by using natural language as a common cross-modal ground. It adds modality encoders (TVL tactile encoder for DIGIT images; audio-to-spectrogram processed as an image via a ResNet26) and tokenizes all modalities into a shared transformer backbone. Two auxiliary language-grounding losses are introduced alongside the behavior cloning loss: (1) a CLIP-style multimodal contrastive loss that builds an observation embedding (via a multi-head attention fusion over modalities) and aligns it with every instruction formed from available modality annotations; (2) a multimodal generative language loss that uses a single transformer head with modality tokens to generate the appropriate instruction from each modality combination. The final objective is L = L_BC + β L_gen + λ L_contrast, and instruction space is broadened via LLM rephrasing of templated annotations. This language-grounded finetuning unlocks zero-shot multimodal prompting, cross-modal prompting, and compositional reasoning, and applies across architectures (Octo and a 3B PaliGemma-based VLA), improving success rates by over 20% vs. baselines.

1. Doing More with Less: Data Augmentation for Sudanese Dialect Automatic Speech Recognition (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Although many Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems have been developed for Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) and Dialectal Arabic (DA), few studies have focused on dialect-specific implementations, particularly for low-resource Arabic dialects such as Sudanese. This paper presents a comprehensive study of data augmentation techniques for fine-tuning OpenAI Whisper models and establishes the first benchmark for the Sudanese dialect. Two augmentation strategies are investigated: (1) self-training with pseudo-labels generated from unlabeled speech, and (2) TTS-based augmentation using synthetic speech from the Klaam TTS system. The best-performing model, Whisper-Medium fine-tuned with combined self-training and TTS augmentation (28.4 hours), achieves a Word Error Rate (WER) of 57.1% on the evaluation set and 51.6% on an out-of-domain holdout set substantially outperforming zero-shot multilingual Whisper (78.8% WER) and MSA-specialized Arabic models (73.8-123% WER). All experiments used low-cost resources (Kaggle free tier and Lightning.ai trial), demonstrating that strategic data augmentation can overcome resource limitations for low-resource dialects and provide a practical roadmap for developing ASR systems for low-resource Arabic dialects and other marginalized language varieties. The models, evaluation benchmarks, and reproducible training pipelines are publicly released to facilitate future research on low-resource Arabic ASR.

2. Few-Shot Accent Synthesis for ASR with LLM-Guided Phoneme Editing ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Accented automatic speech recognition (ASR) often degrades due to the limited availability of accented training data. Prior work has explored accent modeling in low-resource settings, but existing approaches typically require minutes to hours of labeled speech, which may still be impractical for truly scarce accent scenarios. We propose a pipeline that adapts a text-to-speech (TTS) decoder to a target-accent speaker using fewer than ten reference utterances and employs large language model (LLM)-based phoneme editing to generate accent-conditioned pronunciations. The resulting synthetic speech is used to fine-tune a self-supervised ASR model. Experiments demonstrate consistent word error rate (WER) reductions on real accented speech, including cross-speaker evaluation and ultra-low data regimes. A matched-rate random phoneme baseline shows that phoneme-space perturbation itself is a strong form of augmentation, while LLM-guided edits provide additional gains through accent-conditioned structure.

1. UniSpeech: Unified Speech Representation Learning with Labeled and Unlabeled Data (International Conference on Machine Learning 2021, cites: 140)
   In this paper, we propose a unified pre-training approach called UniSpeech to learn speech representations with both unlabeled and labeled data, in which supervised phonetic CTC learning and phonetically-aware contrastive self-supervised learning are conducted in a multi-task learning manner. The resultant representations can capture information more correlated with phonetic structures and improve the generalization across languages and domains. We evaluate the effectiveness of UniSpeech for cross-lingual representation learning on public CommonVoice corpus. The results show that UniSpeech outperforms self-supervised pretraining and supervised transfer learning for speech recognition by a maximum of 13.4% and 17.8% relative phone error rate reductions respectively (averaged over all testing languages). The transferability of UniSpeech is also demonstrated on a domain-shift speech recognition task, i.e., a relative word error rate reduction of 6% against the previous approach.

2. TranUSR: Phoneme-to-word Transcoder Based Unified Speech Representation Learning for Cross-lingual Speech Recognition (Interspeech 2023, cites: 8)
   UniSpeech has achieved superior performance in cross-lingual automatic speech recognition (ASR) by explicitly aligning latent representations to phoneme units using multi-task self-supervised learning. While the learned representations transfer well from high-resource to low-resource languages, predicting words directly from these phonetic representations in downstream ASR is challenging. In this paper, we propose TranUSR, a two-stage model comprising a pre-trained UniData2vec and a phoneme-to-word Transcoder. Different from UniSpeech, UniData2vec replaces the quantized discrete representations with continuous and contextual representations from a teacher model for phonetically-aware pre-training. Then, Transcoder learns to translate phonemes to words with the aid of extra texts, enabling direct word generation. Experiments on Common Voice show that UniData2vec reduces PER by 5.3% compared to UniSpeech, while Transcoder yields a 14.4% WER reduction compared to grapheme fine-tuning.

Thought: Let me search for work on combining TTS-generated synthetic data with semi-supervised/self-training approaches for low-resource ASR, which is a key aspect of the proposed pipeline.
Query: TTS synthetic data semi-supervised self-training low-resource ASR data augmentation speaker diversity

Round 8/10.

Last search results:
1. Improving Code-Switching Speech Recognition with TTS Data Augmentation (Asia-Pacific Signal and Information Processing Association Annual Summit and Conference 2025, cites: 0)
   Automatic speech recognition (ASR) for conversational code-switching speech remains challenging due to the scarcity of realistic, high-quality labeled speech data. This paper explores multilingual text-to-speech (TTS) models as an effective data augmentation technique to address this shortage. Specifically, we fine-tune the multilingual CosyVoice2 TTS model on the SEAME dataset to generate synthetic conversational Chinese-English code-switching speech, significantly increasing the quantity and speaker diversity of available training data. Our experiments demonstrate that augmenting real speech with synthetic speech reduces the mixed error rate (MER) from 12.1 % to 10.1 % on DevMan and from 17.8 % to 16.0 % on DevSGE, indicating performance gains. These results confirm that multilingual TTS is an effective and practical tool for enhancing ASR robustness in low-resource, conversational code-switching scenarios.

2. Semi-supervised ASR based on Iterative Joint Training with Discrete Speech Synthesis (Asia-Pacific Signal and Information Processing Association Annual Summit and Conference 2022, cites: 1)
   This paper proposes Iterative Joint Training (IJT) with discrete speech synthesis for semi-supervised ASR. Thanks to the recent advances in Text-to-Speech synthesis, synthesized speech has become near human-level. However, a large amount of paired speech and text is indispensable to training a high quality TTS model. That prevents low-resource ASR from using TTS for data augmentation. To overcome that problem, we propose to use discrete speech representations, which may be easier to synthesize than continuous representations. The proposed method trains two models from the initial paired data; a speech recognition model whose input is discrete speech representation and a speech synthesis model that generates discrete speech from texts. Both models are repeatedly updated by pseudo-pair data generated by unpaired data using the previous models. Experimental results showed the effectiveness of the proposed method in low-resource settings. It successfully improved recognition performance by using discrete speech representations instead of conventional acoustic features in IJT experiments with a single-speaker speech corpus. Furthermore, the method improved the performance of multi-speaker speech recognition that used only single speaker pair data, unpaired multi-speaker speech, and unpaired text data from the conventional IJT approach using the conventional acoustic features.

3. A Unified Denoising and Adaptation Framework for Self-Supervised Bengali Dialectal ASR (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) for Bengali, the world's fifth most spoken language, remains a significant challenge, critically hindering technological accessibility for its over 270 million speakers. This challenge is compounded by two persistent and intertwined factors: the language's vast dialectal diversity and the prevalence of acoustic noise in real-world environments. While state-of-the-art self-supervised learning (SSL) models have advanced ASR for low-resource languages, they often lack explicit mechanisms to handle environmental noise during pre-training or specialized adaptation strategies for the complex phonetic and lexical variations across Bengali dialects. This paper introduces a novel, unified framework designed to address these dual challenges simultaneously. Our approach is founded on the WavLM model, which is uniquely pre-trained with a masked speech denoising objective, making it inherently robust to acoustic distortions. We propose a specialized multi-stage fine-tuning strategy that first adapts the model to general-domain standard Bengali to establish a strong linguistic foundation and subsequently specializes it for noise-robust dialectal recognition through targeted data augmentation. The framework is rigorously evaluated on a comprehensive benchmark comprising multiple Bengali dialects under a wide range of simulated noisy conditions, from clean audio to low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) levels. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms strong baselines, including standard fine-tuned wav2vec 2.0 and the large-scale multilingual Whisper model. This work establishes a new state-of-the-art for this task and provides a scalable, effective blueprint for developing practical ASR systems for other low-resource, high-variation languages globally.

4. Speaker Augmentation for Low Resource Speech Recognition (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing 2020, cites: 39)
   Text-to-speech synthesis (TTS) has been used as a data augmentation approach for automatic speech recognition (ASR), leveraging additional texts for ASR training. However, in low resource tasks, usually only a limited number of speakers are available, leading to the lack of speaker variations in synthetic speech. In this paper, we propose a novel speaker augmentation approach which can synthesize data with sufficient speaker and text diversity. Here, an end-to-end TTS system is trained with speaker representations from a variational auto-encoder (VAE), which enables TTS to synthesize speech from unseen new speakers via sampling from the trained latent distribution. As a new type of data augmentation approach, speaker augmentation can be combined with traditional feature augmentation approaches, such as SpecAugment. Experiments on a switchboard task show that, given 50 hours of data, the proposed speaker augmentation with SpecAugment significantly reduces word error rate (WER) by 30% relative compared to the system without any data augmentation, and about 18% relative compared to the system with SpecAugment.

5. Few-Shot Accent Synthesis for ASR with LLM-Guided Phoneme Editing ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Accented automatic speech recognition (ASR) often degrades due to the limited availability of accented training data. Prior work has explored accent modeling in low-resource settings, but existing approaches typically require minutes to hours of labeled speech, which may still be impractical for truly scarce accent scenarios. We propose a pipeline that adapts a text-to-speech (TTS) decoder to a target-accent speaker using fewer than ten reference utterances and employs large language model (LLM)-based phoneme editing to generate accent-conditioned pronunciations. The resulting synthetic speech is used to fine-tune a self-supervised ASR model. Experiments demonstrate consistent word error rate (WER) reductions on real accented speech, including cross-speaker evaluation and ultra-low data regimes. A matched-rate random phoneme baseline shows that phoneme-space perturbation itself is a strong form of augmentation, while LLM-guided edits provide additional gains through accent-conditioned structure.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Speech recognition for low-resource languages or specialized
domains lacks transcribed audio; how can we leverage synthetic
data or transfer learning to reach competitive accuracy with
minimal real labeled data?


### Proposed method
1) Build the right text and language model first. Retrieve and transform in-domain text using a dataset-retrieval-and-transformation pipeline: mine public corpora, manuals, chat logs, and domain documents that match the target language/domain; then normalize to ASR style (spoken-form punctuation, numeral expansion, abbreviations, code-switch variants) and expand with rare terms and morphology. From this script, construct a pronunciation lexicon or grapheme mapping and train a compact domain language model (e.g., 4–6 gram and a small transformer LM) for shallow fusion or WFST decoding. Deduplicate and stratify the script for coverage over word frequency bins, numerics, entities, and prosody markers.

2) Turn the script into strong synthetic audio with transfer-learned multilingual TTS. Start from a robust multilingual TTS trained on high-resource languages; fine-tune lightly toward the target language or accent using any available transcribed minutes or pseudo-transcripts, leveraging codec-based acoustic tokenization to preserve cross-lingual prosody and phonotactics. Synthesize 100–1000 hours with multiple speakers, speaking rates, and style/noise profiles; augment on-the-fly with room impulse responses, codecs, and gain perturbations. Ensure phoneme and syllable coverage by sampling the script with a curriculum that upweights rare words and domain terms early and ramps toward natural frequency later.

3) Pretrain the ASR encoder with a unified supervised plus self-supervised objective. Use a wav2vec 2.0–style stack with a quantizer and transformer context network. Jointly optimize (i) CTC on high-resource labeled speech and on a quality-controlled subset of the synthetic speech-text pairs to encourage phone-aligned features, and (ii) masked contrastive learning on large amounts of unlabeled target audio mixed with the remaining synthetic audio to adapt the codebook and contextual space to the target acoustics and vocabulary. After convergence, freeze the feature encoder or apply a low learning rate to stabilize downstream tuning.

4) Fine-tune with minimal real labels, strong pseudo-labels, and a calibrated curriculum. Use a strong multilingual teacher ASR to transcribe the unlabeled target audio; keep only high-confidence segments and filter with the domain lexicon and LM to reduce homophone and OOV errors. Train the student on a mixture of the small real labeled set, high-confidence pseudo-labels, and a downweighted sample of synthetic pairs. Start with real+pseudo data, anneal synthetic weight as WER drops, and gradually admit lower-confidence pseudo-labels if the dev set improves. Use CTC or RNN-T loss with SpecAugment and speed perturbation; add parameter-efficient adapters or selectively unfreeze upper transformer layers for domain adaptation. Decode with beam search and shallow-fuse the domain LM; optionally rescore with the transformer LM. Iterate: re-transcribe unlabeled audio with the improved student, refresh the LM on new transcripts, and regenerate a small tranche of harder synthetic utterances focused on remaining error modes until dev WER plateaus.

### Rationale
Domain-matched text determines the vocabulary and n-gram statistics that drive ASR decoding; retrieving and transforming existing datasets yields high-coverage scripts without costly manual authoring. High-fidelity multilingual TTS, trained via transfer, can then turn these scripts into acoustically diverse, label-perfect pairs that inject the right terms, numbers, and entities into the acoustic model and LM, even when human transcripts are scarce.

Unified supervised plus self-supervised pretraining aligns the encoder to phonetic structure while adapting its latent space to target acoustics using abundant unlabeled audio. This produces features that transfer with little labeled data. Finally, confidence-filtered pseudo-labeling exploits all available target audio to close the domain gap, while curriculum weighting and parameter-efficient adaptation prevent overfitting to synthetic artifacts. Shallow-fusion with the domain LM ensures the decoder respects target phrasing and terminology, pushing accuracy toward competitive levels with only a small real labeled set.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] A Thorough Examination of Decoding Methods in the Era of LLMs (2402.06925)
   problem: Practitioners lack clear, LLM-era guidance for selecting decoding methods across diverse tasks and alignment settings; many prior conclusions were drawn on task-specific models and may not transfer.
   method:  Conduct a comprehensive benchmark of deterministic (greedy, beam search, diverse beam search, contrastive search, contrastive decoding, FSD/FSD-d, DoLa) and stochastic (temperature, top-p, top-k, η, mirostat, typical) decoding across coding, math, summarization, translation, commonsense, factuality, instruction-following, and open-ended generation on unaligned/aligned Llama-2-7B and other models. Findings: deterministic methods generally outperform stochastic ones on closed-ended tasks, while stochastic methods excel in open-ended generation (e.g., greedy/beam/DBS underperform on MAUVE) (lines 127–131, 114). Alignment narrows performance gaps and reduces dependence on decoding (RDP drops; entropy lower for aligned models, Table 2) (lines 133–138, 141–147). Deterministic methods reduce hallucinations and improve instruction following (higher FActScore and AlpacaEval vs stochastic) (line 139). Among stochastic methods, low-temperature sampling is typically best on closed-ended tasks (line 148). Efficiency analysis shows FSD/FSD-d and stochastic methods run near greedy speed, while contrastive search is slowest and contrastive decoding adds a constant overhead; beam/DBS incur moderate, length-growing latency (lines 154–166).

2. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Transformer self-attention in vision foundation models fails to highlight domain-specific spatial relations under large distribution shifts to specialized imagery, limiting transfer.
   method:  Learn a small set of global attention maps shared across images and tokens (e.g., C=10) that encode reusable domain-specific patterns (e.g., horizontal/vertical context for circuits, local neighborhoods for maps). For each token at location (i, j), crop an aligned h×h sub-map from each global map (top-left at (h−i, h−j)), normalize to form token-to-token attention, apply it to values V, and mix the result with the original multi-head attention via learned weights r and balance α, inserting this module in a single mid transformer layer (e.g., 16th). This adds minimal parameters, reduces overfitting, and consistently improves over parameter-efficient transfer baselines like LoRA/AdaptFormer on low-resource tasks.

3. [MULTIMODAL] OFA: Unifying Architectures, Tasks, and Modalities through a Simple Sequence-to-Sequence Learning Framework (2202.03052)
   problem: Multimodal and language models typically rely on task-specific heads/adapters and heterogeneous task formulations, and often entangle visual representations with downstream detectors, hindering zero-shot transfer and true unification across tasks and modalities.
   method:  OFA casts all uni- and cross-modal tasks as instruction-driven sequence-to-sequence generation with a single encoder–decoder Transformer and no task- or modality-specific layers. It builds a shared multimodal token vocabulary by discretizing images into short code sequences and representing objects as label tokens plus discretized bounding-box coordinates, while encoding images via ResNet patch features. Handcrafted instructions unify task interfaces across pretraining, finetuning, and inference, enabling a task- and modality-agnostic I/O and architecture.

4. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [ROBOTICS] RREx-BoT: Remote Referring Expressions with a Bag of Tricks (2301.12614)
   problem: Generic vision–language models are trained on 2D images and lack mechanisms to ground language to objects situated in 3D embodied environments, where spatial relations and local context matter for remote object grounding.
   method:  Adapt a pretrained VL backbone (ViLBERT) to 3D by replacing 2D box encodings with 3D region positional embeddings (x, y, z, r) normalized relative to the observing viewpoint, with depth estimated from the median depth inside each region. Enrich local context by adding Mask-RCNN proposal regions and average features from neighboring viewpoints to each viewpoint batch. Fine-tune with a sigmoid/BCE objective over regions within a sampled viewpoint and apply viewpoint-level negative augmentation (random viewpoints without the target) to suppress false positives. These 3D encodings and context cues significantly improve grounding, as shown by ablations removing 3D encoding and context proposals that reduce RGS/SR (Tables 3–4).

7. [CV] S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging (2412.13156)
   problem: Medical image segmentation models trained on scarce, low-diversity data often learn biased, noisy semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness. Existing fixes (specialized augmentations/architectures) rely on domain assumptions that may not generalize across modalities (CT, MRI, RGB).
   method:  Introduce Semantic Stacking (S2S2), an add-on, domain-agnostic training strategy that estimates a denoised semantic representation by stacking images that share the same ground-truth segmentation map and guiding the network toward that representation. Practically, S2S2 adds a semantic consistency loss between features from two images per mask sampled from the stack and combines it with the standard segmentation loss. The consistency loss is applied to both encoder and decoder features and instantiated as 1 − cosine similarity, improving feature robustness without changing the base architecture or augmentation pipeline. (Paper: Sec. 1, 3; Fig. 2; lines 29–31, 55–74, 89–95, 123–126; Code: TransUNet trainer.py utils.ConsistencyLoss)

8. [CV] Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (2107.11787)
   problem: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation trained with only image-level labels relies on sparse CAM seeds and off-the-shelf saliency maps that are used offline and not adapted to the target data, yielding low-quality pseudo labels and coarse boundaries.
   method:  AuxSegNet is a joint multi-task framework that treats semantic segmentation as the primary task and leverages multi-label image classification and saliency detection as auxiliary tasks, all sharing a backbone and trained end-to-end using only image-level labels and pre-trained saliency maps as initial pseudo labels. The classification branch produces CAMs; the saliency and segmentation branches use atrous conv decoders and are trained with losses on both initial and refined predictions against pseudo labels. Saliency pseudo labels are updated online by fusing the model’s refined saliency with pre-trained saliency through a dense CRF, while segmentation pseudo labels are generated from CAMs (and later their affinity-refined versions), enabling the auxiliary tasks to adapt to the target dataset and improve segmentation performance iteratively.

9. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Transformer self-attention in vision foundation models fails to highlight domain-specific spatial relations under large distribution shifts to specialized imagery, limiting transfer.
   method:  Learn a small set of global attention maps shared across images and tokens (e.g., C=10) that encode reusable domain-specific patterns (e.g., horizontal/vertical context for circuits, local neighborhoods for maps). For each token at location (i, j), crop an aligned h×h sub-map from each global map (top-left at (h−i, h−j)), normalize to form token-to-token attention, apply it to values V, and mix the result with the original multi-head attention via learned weights r and balance α, inserting this module in a single mid transformer layer (e.g., 16th). This adds minimal parameters, reduces overfitting, and consistently improves over parameter-efficient transfer baselines like LoRA/AdaptFormer on low-resource tasks.

10. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

11. [RL] Towards Theoretical Understanding of Inverse Reinforcement Learning (2304.12966)
   problem: Estimating the full set of rewards that make an expert policy optimal (the feasible reward set) lacks a principled accuracy metric and stability guarantees with respect to estimation errors in the environment and expert policy.
   method:  Introduce a Hausdorff-distance–based evaluation H_d between feasible reward sets induced by the L∞ metric on rewards (d_G), and define a premetric ρ_G on IRL problems that combines L1 distance between transition models and differences in the support of the expert policy. Prove a Lipschitz continuity result (Theorem 3.2) showing that, with unrestricted bounded rewards, small changes in the IRL problem (per ρ_G) imply small Hausdorff changes in feasible reward sets; and show via counterexamples that common restrictions (state-only, time-homogeneous, β-margin rewards) break this Lipschitz property.

12. [RL] Demonstration-Guided Reinforcement Learning with Efficient Exploration for Task Automation of Surgical Robot (2302.09772)
   problem: Off-policy RL for surgical robot automation struggles with exploration when only a modest set of demonstrations is available. Prior demo-regularized actor-only methods suffer from critic overestimation that assigns high values to expert-unlike actions, hindering efficient exploration.
   method:  Introduce an expert-guided actor-critic framework that regularizes both the critic and the actor using an action-dissimilarity term to expert actions. Concretely, augment the reward with a behavior gap penalty and define a regularized Q-function Qπ(s,a)=E[∑γ^t (rt − γα d(at+1, a^e_{t+1}))], so the critic lowers value estimates for expert-unlike actions while the actor maximizes Q plus a similarity term to expert behavior. This joint regularization mitigates critic overestimation and explicitly steers exploration toward expert-like actions, improving sample efficiency. (Supported: paper III-A lines 58–72; lines 29–33; code dex/agents/dex.py update_critic/update_actor)

13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

14. [IR_REC] Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval (2509.09459)
   problem: Contrastive fine-tuning of multilingual dense retrievers is hindered by false negatives in hard-negative sets, since top-k retrieved candidates often include relevant but unjudged documents.
   method:  Build a multilingual hard-negative candidate list by fusing representations from multiple retrievers (e.g., mE5, BGE) via a projection-and-concatenation fusion to score candidates, then use an LLM to judge each candidate’s relevance to the query and its positive (0/1/2 for irrelevant/partially/highly relevant) and keep only those judged irrelevant. This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).

15. [IR_REC] Semi-supervised Collaborative Filtering by Text-enhanced Domain Adaptation (2007.07085)
   problem: Cross-domain recommendation on sparse implicit-feedback data without user/item overlap suffers from severe data sparsity and label noise from negative sampling; directly aligning latent embeddings across domains is sub-optimal because different datasets induce different latent spaces.
   method:  Propose TDAR, a text-enhanced domain adaptation framework that uses domain-invariant textual features from reviews as anchors to align source and target. It concatenates fixed textual features with trainable user/item embeddings and feeds them to domain classifiers; embeddings are adversarially trained to confuse the classifiers so user/item representations align across domains in a shared latent-textual space (with separate user/item adaptation nets). On the target domain, training uses only positive labels plus the domain-adaptation signal, abandoning negative sampling to avoid label noise, while the denser source domain supplies negative supervision. This semi-supervised scheme transfers distributional patterns from a dense source to a sparse target and mitigates mislabeling on the target.

16. [SPEECH] UniSpeech: Unified Speech Representation Learning with Labeled and Unlabeled Data (2101.07597)
   problem: Low-resource ASR lacks labeled data, while purely supervised transfer ignores abundant unlabeled target audio and purely self-supervised pretraining may not learn phoneme-aligned, SR-specific features that transfer well across languages and domains.
   method:  Pretrain a wav2vec 2.0-style encoder with a unified multitask objective that combines supervised phonetic CTC and phonetically-aware contrastive self-supervision. On labeled source data, jointly optimize CTC to align contextual representations with phoneme labels and a masked contrastive loss between contextual embeddings and quantized latent targets; on unlabeled target data, apply the same contrastive objective to adapt the codebook and contextual space. The architecture uses a convolutional feature encoder, a Transformer context network, and a Gumbel-softmax vector quantizer with codebook diversity regularization, followed by fine-tuning (freezing the feature encoder) on small labeled target sets.

17. [SPEECH] Bahasa Harmony: A Comprehensive Dataset for Bahasa Text-to-Speech Synthesis with Discrete Codec Modeling of EnGen-TTS. (2410.06608)
   problem: Achieving strong multilingual TTS performance, especially for low-resource languages, with a model that transfers well across languages.
   method:  The system leverages transfer learning: models are first trained on LJSpeech and VCTK to learn text–audio alignments, then fine-tuned on target language data. The mT5-based text conditioning and codec LM enable effective cross-lingual synthesis. Evaluations across seven languages (Spanish, Portuguese, German, Dutch, Hindi, Marathi, Tamil) show consistent MOS improvements over VITS and NaturalSpeech, including large gains in low-resource Indic languages, demonstrating robustness and adaptability of the approach.

18. [LLM_NLP] Better Synthetic Data by Retrieving and Transforming Existing Datasets (2404.14361)
   problem: High-quality, task-specific NLP training data is often scarce. Purely LLM-generated synthetic datasets tend to lack diversity and difficulty, while directly using existing datasets can be misaligned with the target task format and objectives.
   method:  DataTune automatically repurposes public datasets for a new task via two stages: (1) dual-stage dataset retrieval from HuggingFace—first using a bi-encoder retriever (DataFinder) to propose candidates, then an LLM-based reranker that considers dataset descriptions, schema, and sample rows, with self-consistency to select robust datasets; and (2) an LLM-driven transformation pipeline with Task Expansion (enrich task spec), Schema Selection (identify relevant columns), a Planning Module (produce a step-by-step transformation plan), and an Execution Module (apply the plan per row) to generate task-aligned input/output examples. Transforming multiple top-ranked datasets preserves source diversity and yields harder, less-duplicated examples while maintaining label accuracy, leading to consistent fine-tuning gains over few-shot prompting and over using synthetic or retrieved data alone.

19. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.
   method:  Introduce a two-loop framework where an outer loop places a physical fixture to scaffold the task and an inner loop learns the manipulation policy. The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses. The inner loop uses a vision-based RL policy (variant of A3C) from RGB images to end-effector motions executed via operational space control, yielding dramatically faster learning and higher success on peg insertion, wrench manipulation, and shallow-depth insertion.

20. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

21. [ROBOTICS] Beyond Sight: Finetuning Generalist Robot Policies with Heterogeneous Sensors via Language Grounding (2501.04693)
   problem: Generalist robot policies are largely trained on vision and proprioception and lack semantic grounding for scarce but crucial modalities like touch and audio. Naively finetuning on small heterogeneous-sensor datasets causes the policy to ignore new sensors and fails to enable zero-shot multimodal reasoning in partially observable settings.
   method:  FuSe finetunes pre-trained generalist policies by using natural language as a common cross-modal ground. It adds modality encoders (TVL tactile encoder for DIGIT images; audio-to-spectrogram processed as an image via a ResNet26) and tokenizes all modalities into a shared transformer backbone. Two auxiliary language-grounding losses are introduced alongside the behavior cloning loss: (1) a CLIP-style multimodal contrastive loss that builds an observation embedding (via a multi-head attention fusion over modalities) and aligns it with every instruction formed from available modality annotations; (2) a multimodal generative language loss that uses a single transformer head with modality tokens to generate the appropriate instruction from each modality combination. The final objective is L = L_BC + β L_gen + λ L_contrast, and instruction space is broadened via LLM rephrasing of templated annotations. This language-grounded finetuning unlocks zero-shot multimodal prompting, cross-modal prompting, and compositional reasoning, and applies across architectures (Octo and a 3B PaliGemma-based VLA), improving success rates by over 20% vs. baselines.

We evaluate the effectiveness of UniSpeech for cross-lingual representation learning on public CommonVoice corpus. Different from UniSpeech, UniData2vec replaces the quantized discrete representations with continuous and contextual representations from a teacher model for phonetically-aware pre-training. Then, Transcoder learns to translate phonemes to words with the aid of extra texts, enabling direct word generation. Experiments on Common Voice show that UniData2vec reduces PER by 5.3% compared to UniSpeech, while Transcoder yields a 14.4% WER reduction compared to grapheme fine-tuning.

   This paper proposes Iterative Joint Training (IJT) with discrete speech synthesis for semi-supervised ASR. Thanks to the recent advances in Text-to-Speech synthesis, synthesized speech has become near human-level. However, a large amount of paired speech and text is indispensable to training a high quality TTS model. That prevents low-resource ASR from using TTS for data augmentation. To overcome that problem, we propose to use discrete speech representations, which may be easier to synthesize than continuous representations. The proposed method trains two models from the initial paired data; a speech recognition model whose input is discrete speech representation and a speech synthesis model that generates discrete speech from texts. Both models are repeatedly updated by pseudo-pair data generated by unpaired data using the previous models. Experimental results showed the effectiveness of the proposed method in low-resource settings. It successfully improved recognition performance by using discrete speech representations instead of conventional acoustic features in IJT experiments with a single-speaker speech corpus. Furthermore, the method improved the performance of multi-speaker speech recognition that used only single speaker pair data, unpaired multi-speaker speech, and unpaired text data from the conventional IJT approach using the conventional acoustic features.
