messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Speech recognition for low-resource languages or specialized
domains lacks transcribed audio; how can we leverage synthetic
data or transfer learning to reach competitive accuracy with
minimal real labeled data?


### Proposed method
We propose RAPSODY, a low-label ASR training recipe that pairs a structure-aware encoder with two nonstandard supervision signals and a look-ahead synthetic curriculum. First, we insert a Global Temporal Prior Attention (GTPA) module into a mid layer of a Conformer/Transformer encoder. GTPA learns a small bank of reusable attention templates over time that encode language- and domain-agnostic temporal relations (e.g., local context, syllable-scale spans, onset–nucleus–coda and long-range vowel/consonant dependencies). For each frame t, we crop an aligned h×h sub-map from each global template, normalize it into token-to-token weights, apply it to values, and mix the result with multi-head self-attention via learned gates. This adds few parameters, regularizes attention toward speech-appropriate structures, and reduces overfitting in low-resource transfer.

Second, we replace standard pseudo-label pretraining with Rendition-Stack Consistency. For each text segment, we form a stack of heterogeneous audio renditions: diverse multi-speaker TTS, speed/pitch/noise perturbations, and optional voice-converted in-domain utterances aligned to that text. We enforce a denoising consistency objective that pulls encoder and decoder hidden states for all items in a stack toward a shared text-conditioned prototype (computed as the moving-average feature of a random pair in the stack), using a cosine similarity loss at multiple layers. The supervised head (CTC or transducer) is trained on the small real labeled set only; stacks do not require frame-level alignments. This consolidates speaker, channel, and prosody variation while keeping content fixed, yielding invariant features that transfer across accents and domains with minimal labels.

Third, we introduce Listwise Hypothesis Distillation without ground truth to exploit unlabeled in-domain audio. The current ASR produces n-best hypotheses per utterance. A composite scorer (domain LM, pronunciation/lexicon prior, and an LLM judge scoring semantic plausibility for the target domain) induces a strict ranking over the n-best. We optimize the acoustic model and fusion weights with a differentiable listwise loss (e.g., weighted RankNet/Plackett–Luce) so that the model’s scores respect the external ranking. To avoid hurting partially correct hypotheses, we filter “negatives” with an LLM that flags hypotheses containing correct substrings/entities as ambiguous rather than incorrect; only truly irrelevant hypotheses contribute as negatives. This converts readily-available priors into powerful, label-free supervision that goes beyond top-1 pseudo-labels.

Finally, we drive synthetic data generation with Look-ahead Curriculum Planning. We define coverage goals (“skills”) over phoneme n-grams, tones/prosody patterns, morphology, and domain entity pronunciation variants. Simple regressors estimate the expected WER improvement from adding a candidate synthetic batch given the model’s current confusion profile and the batch’s coverage statistics. A short-horizon tree search samples generation actions (speaker/pace/noise profiles, lexicon variants, entity lists), prunes low-value branches via success predictors, and chooses the next synthetic batch to materialize from TTS/VC. We alternate planning–generation–training rounds, annealing the synthetic mix as the model’s uncertainty shrinks. Throughout, GTPA parameters are shared across languages and updated jointly, enabling cross-lingual transfer of temporal priors even when labeled target data is scarce.

Training schedule: warm-start the encoder on any public speech with masked prediction only to stabilize features; insert GTPA and fine-tune briefly on the small labeled set to set the supervised head; then alternate (a) Rendition-Stack Consistency on synthetic stacks, (b) Listwise Hypothesis Distillation on unlabeled in-domain audio, and (c) Look-ahead Curriculum Planning to choose the next synthetic tranche. Decode with a light domain LM (or shallow fusion) during the listwise phase; remove or down-weight it at the end if end-to-end decoding is desired. This end-to-end process reaches competitive accuracy with minimal real labels by combining structure priors, label-free listwise supervision, and planned synthetic coverage.

### Rationale
GTPA injects reusable, speech-appropriate attention structure into the encoder, improving generalization when labeled target data is limited and reducing the burden on data to teach long- and mid-range temporal dependencies. Rendition-Stack Consistency exploits abundant synthetic variation anchored to identical text to explicitly disentangle content from nuisance factors, providing stronger invariance than standard augmentation alone while consuming no additional labels. Listwise Hypothesis Distillation turns readily available external knowledge (domain LM, lexicon, LLM semantic judgments) into a dense supervision signal on unlabeled audio, leveraging the full n-best list rather than brittle top-1 pseudo-labels and guarding against false-negative penalties.

The look-ahead synthetic curriculum addresses the core inefficiency of naive TTS augmentation: not all synthetic utterances are equally useful. By planning over phonetic/prosodic/entity coverage with simple, learned improvement predictors, the system preferentially generates batches that close the model’s current weaknesses, yielding faster gains under a fixed synthetic budget. These mechanisms are complementary: structure priors ease transfer, stack consistency builds robust representations, listwise distillation aligns scoring to domain semantics without labels, and planning ensures synthetic data is targeted, together enabling competitive ASR with minimal real supervision.

1. [LLM_NLP] A Thorough Examination of Decoding Methods in the Era of LLMs (2402.06925)
   problem: Practitioners lack clear, LLM-era guidance for selecting decoding methods across diverse tasks and alignment settings; many prior conclusions were drawn on task-specific models and may not transfer.
   method:  Conduct a comprehensive benchmark of deterministic (greedy, beam search, diverse beam search, contrastive search, contrastive decoding, FSD/FSD-d, DoLa) and stochastic (temperature, top-p, top-k, η, mirostat, typical) decoding across coding, math, summarization, translation, commonsense, factuality, instruction-following, and open-ended generation on unaligned/aligned Llama-2-7B and other models. Findings: deterministic methods generally outperform stochastic ones on closed-ended tasks, while stochastic methods excel in open-ended generation (e.g., greedy/beam/DBS underperform on MAUVE) (lines 127–131, 114). Alignment narrows performance gaps and reduces dependence on decoding (RDP drops; entropy lower for aligned models, Table 2) (lines 133–138, 141–147). Deterministic methods reduce hallucinations and improve instruction following (higher FActScore and AlpacaEval vs stochastic) (line 139). Among stochastic methods, low-temperature sampling is typically best on closed-ended tasks (line 148). Efficiency analysis shows FSD/FSD-d and stochastic methods run near greedy speed, while contrastive search is slowest and contrastive decoding adds a constant overhead; beam/DBS incur moderate, length-growing latency (lines 154–166).

2. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Transformer self-attention in vision foundation models fails to highlight domain-specific spatial relations under large distribution shifts to specialized imagery, limiting transfer.

For each token at location (i, j), crop an aligned h×h sub-map from each global map (top-left at (h−i, h−j)), normalize to form token-to-token attention, apply it to values V, and mix the result with the original multi-head attention via learned weights r and balance α, inserting this module in a single mid transformer layer (e.g., 16th). This adds minimal parameters, reduces overfitting, and consistently improves over parameter-efficient transfer baselines like LoRA/AdaptFormer on low-resource tasks.

Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

   problem: Estimating the full set of rewards that make an expert policy optimal (the feasible reward set) lacks a principled accuracy metric and stability guarantees with respect to estimation errors in the environment and expert policy.

1. MPSA-Conformer-CTC/Attention: A High-Accuracy, Low-Complexity End-to-End Approach for Tibetan Speech Recognition (Italian National Conference on Sensors 2024, cites: 3)
   This study addresses the challenges of low accuracy and high computational demands in Tibetan speech recognition by investigating the application of end-to-end networks. We propose a decoding strategy that integrates Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) and Attention mechanisms, capitalizing on the benefits of automatic alignment and attention weight extraction. The Conformer architecture is utilized as the encoder, leading to the development of the Conformer-CTC/Attention model. This model first extracts global features from the speech signal using the Conformer, followed by joint decoding of these features through CTC and Attention mechanisms. To mitigate convergence issues during training, particularly with longer input feature sequences, we introduce a Probabilistic Sparse Attention mechanism within the joint CTC/Attention framework. Additionally, we implement a maximum entropy optimization algorithm for CTC, effectively addressing challenges such as increased path counts, spike distributions, and local optima during training. We designate the proposed method as the MaxEnt-Optimized Probabilistic Sparse Attention Conformer-CTC/Attention Model (MPSA-Conformer-CTC/Attention). Experimental results indicate that our improved model achieves a word error rate reduction of 10.68% and 9.57% on self-constructed and open-source Tibetan datasets, respectively, compared to the baseline model. Furthermore, the enhanced model not only reduces memory consumption and training time but also improves generalization capability and accuracy.

2. Phoneme-Aware Augmentation for Robust Cantonese ASR Under Low-Resource Conditions (Symmetry 2025, cites: 2)
   Cantonese automatic speech recognition (ASR) faces persistent challenges due to its nine lexical tones, extensive phonological variation, and the scarcity of professionally transcribed corpora. To address these issues, we propose a lightweight and data-efficient framework that leverages weak phonetic supervision (WPS) in conjunction with two pho-neme-aware augmentation strategies. (1) Dynamic Boundary-Aligned Phoneme Dropout progressively removes entire IPA segments according to a curriculum schedule, simulating real-world phenomena such as elision, lenition, and tonal drift while ensuring training stability. (2) Phoneme-Aware SpecAugment confines all time- and frequency-masking operations within phoneme boundaries and prioritizes high-attention regions, thereby preserving intra-phonemic contours and formant integrity. Built on the Whistle encoder—which integrates a Conformer backbone, Connectionist Temporal Classification–Conditional Random Field (CTC-CRF) alignment, and a multi-lingual phonetic space—the approach requires only a grapheme-to-phoneme lexicon and Montreal Forced Aligner outputs, without any additional manual labeling. Experiments on the Cantonese subset of Common Voice demonstrate consistent gains: Dynamic Dropout alone reduces phoneme error rate (PER) from 17.8% to 16.7% with 50 h of speech and 16.4% to 15.1% with 100 h, while the combination of the two augmentations further lowers PER to 15.9%/14.4%. 1. Multimodal N-best List Rescoring with Weakly Supervised Pre-training in Hybrid Speech Recognition (Industrial Conference on Data Mining 2021, cites: 3)
   N-best list rescoring, an essential step in hybrid automatic speech recognition (ASR), aims to re-evaluate the N-best hypothesis list decoded by the acoustic model (AM) and language model (LM), and selects the top-ranked hypotheses as the final ASR results. This paper explores the performance of neural rescoring models in scenarios where large-scale training labels are not available. We propose a weakly supervised neural rescoring method, WSNeuRescore, where a listwise multimodal neural rescoring model is pre-trained using labels automatically obtained without human annotators. Specifically, we employ the output of an unsupervised rescoring model, the weighted linear combination of the AM score and the LM score, as a weak supervision signal to pre-train the neural rescoring model. Our experimental evaluations on a public dataset validate that the pre-trained rescoring model based on weakly supervised data leads to an impressive performance. In the extreme scenario without any high-quality labeled data, it achieves up to an 11.90% WER reduction and a 15.56% NDCG@10 improvement over the baseline method in Kaldi, a well-known open-source toolkit in the ASR community.

2. Reference-Free Rating of LLM Responses via Latent Information (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   How reliable are single-response LLM-as-a-judge ratings without references, and can we obtain fine-grained, deterministic scores in this setting? We study the common practice of asking a judge model to assign Likert-scale scores to free-text responses and show two systematic issues: scores are unstable under sampling and poorly calibrated, leading to compression near the top of the scale and frequent ties. We then propose and evaluate Latent Judges, which derive scalar ratings from internal model signals: (i) probability-weighted scores over integer ratings, (ii) verifier-style probabilities of"yes", and (iii) linear probes trained on model activations at the rating position. Across a broad suite of pairwise and single-rating benchmarks, latent methods match or surpass standard prompting, with consistent gains on pairwise accuracy and listwise ranking relevant to Best-of-N selection. Probability-weighted scores achieve the strongest single-rating correlations, while probes recover useful signals when output logits are miscalibrated. These results indicate that latent information provides deterministic and more discriminative signals for reference-free evaluation, and can improve selection and training approaches like Best-of-$N$, multi-teacher distillation, and routing.

3. Large Language Models are Efficient Learners of Noise-Robust Speech Recognition (International Conference on Learning Representations 2024, cites: 41)
   Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have promoted generative error correction (GER) for automatic speech recognition (ASR), which leverages the rich linguistic knowledge and powerful reasoning ability of LLMs to improve recognition results. The latest work proposes a GER benchmark with HyPoradise dataset to learn the mapping from ASR N-best hypotheses to ground-truth transcription by efficient LLM finetuning, which shows great effectiveness but lacks specificity on noise-robust ASR. In this work, we extend the benchmark to noisy conditions and investigate if we can teach LLMs to perform denoising for GER just like what robust ASR do}, where one solution is introducing noise information as a conditioner into LLM. However, directly incorporating noise embeddings from audio encoder could harm the LLM tuning due to cross-modality gap. To this end, we propose to extract a language-space noise embedding from the N-best list to represent the noise conditions of source speech, which can promote the denoising process in GER. Furthermore, in order to enhance its representation ability of audio noise, we design a knowledge distillation (KD) approach via mutual information estimation to distill the real noise information in audio embeddings to our language embedding. Experiments on various latest LLMs demonstrate our approach achieves a new breakthrough with up to 53.9% correction improvement in terms of word error rate while with limited training data. Analysis shows that our language-space noise embedding can well represent the noise conditions of source speech, under which off-the-shelf LLMs show strong ability of language-space denoising.

4. HyPoradise: An Open Baseline for Generative Speech Recognition with Large Language Models (Neural Information Processing Systems 2023, cites: 73)
   Advancements in deep neural networks have allowed automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems to attain human parity on several publicly available clean speech datasets. However, even state-of-the-art ASR systems experience performance degradation when confronted with adverse conditions, as a well-trained acoustic model is sensitive to variations in the speech domain, e.g., background noise. Intuitively, humans address this issue by relying on their linguistic knowledge: the meaning of ambiguous spoken terms is usually inferred from contextual cues thereby reducing the dependency on the auditory system. Inspired by this observation, we introduce the first open-source benchmark to utilize external large language models (LLMs) for ASR error correction, where N-best decoding hypotheses provide informative elements for true transcription prediction. This approach is a paradigm shift from the traditional language model rescoring strategy that can only select one candidate hypothesis as the output transcription. The proposed benchmark contains a novel dataset, HyPoradise (HP), encompassing more than 334,000 pairs of N-best hypotheses and corresponding accurate transcriptions across prevalent speech domains. Given this dataset, we examine three types of error correction techniques based on LLMs with varying amounts of labeled hypotheses-transcription pairs, which gains a significant word error rate (WER) reduction. Experimental evidence demonstrates the proposed technique achieves a breakthrough by surpassing the upper bound of traditional re-ranking based methods. More surprisingly, LLM with reasonable prompt and its generative capability can even correct those tokens that are missing in N-best list. We make our results publicly accessible for reproducible pipelines with released pre-trained models, thus providing a new evaluation paradigm for ASR error correction with LLMs.

response:
Reasoning: The searches reveal no published work that combines all four components of RAPSODY together, nor any single paper that matches even two of the core novel mechanisms jointly applied to ASR. The GTPA module is a direct adaptation of the "Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models" global attention template idea to speech/Conformer encoders — this cross-modal transfer is non-trivial and unsurfaced for ASR. Rendition-Stack Consistency (multi-rendition TTS stacks with cosine consistency loss at multiple layers) has no direct match; the closest is S2S2 for medical segmentation and standard TTS augmentation, but not this specific formulation for ASR. Listwise Hypothesis Distillation using LLM-scored n-best with a differentiable Plackett-Luce/RankNet loss to train the acoustic model (not just rescore) is distinct from HyPoradise/GER correction and WSNeuRescore (which use weak supervision for rescoring, not acoustic model training via listwise loss). Look-ahead curriculum planning for synthetic TTS generation has no surfaced match. The combination of four distinct novel mechanisms — none of which has been jointly published for this problem — matches the Score 5 anchor: new named components not present in surfaced literature, with no end-to-end near-clone found.

Prior works:
  - Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2024): Global attention template mechanism that GTPA directly adapts to speech — same mechanism, different modality/problem.
  - WSNeuRescore / Multimodal N-best List Rescoring with Weakly Supervised Pre-training (2021): Listwise neural rescoring of n-best with weak supervision, but for rescoring only, not acoustic model training via differentiable listwise loss.
  - HyPoradise / LLMs for ASR N-best correction (2023): LLM-based correction of n-best hypotheses, but generative correction paradigm, not listwise distillation into acoustic model.
  - S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation (2024): Stack-based consistency loss across same-label images — analogous to Rendition-Stack Consistency but for vision segmentation, not ASR.
  - UniSpeech (2021): Unified supervised+self-supervised pretraining for low-resource ASR transfer — related training paradigm but no stack consistency or listwise distillation.
Score: 5

messages.content:
You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Speech recognition for low-resource languages or specialized
domains lacks transcribed audio; how can we leverage synthetic
data or transfer learning to reach competitive accuracy with
minimal real labeled data?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed 2112b9511767 [IR_REC] — "GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
- Method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

### Seed a9647aa8fe5e [IR_REC] — "Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
- Method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

### Seed 3917da5fd88a [LLM_NLP] — "Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
- Method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

### Seed 663d598bb0b9 [CV] — "KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
- Method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

### Seed 6bafdc8c3e0c [ROBOTICS] — "Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
- Method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

### Seed 9afe61a7b42b [ROBOTICS] — "Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
- Method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

### Seed 57b1e475a15e [IR_REC] — "Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
- Method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

### Seed ed8619fe7688 [CV] — "Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
- Method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

### Seed c0ef4638400b [ROBOTICS] — "TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
- Method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

### Seed 69f9d4fc5e68 [RL] — "Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
- Method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

### Seed 6d9839993b4e [MULTIMODAL] — "VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection"

- Method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).

### Seed 0b8e0ee039a0 [CV] — "Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
- Method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.

### Seed 3e4700d9e4a8 [LLM_NLP] — "MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.
- Method:  Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Speech recognition for low-resource languages or specialized
domains lacks transcribed audio; how can we leverage synthetic
data or transfer learning to reach competitive accuracy with
minimal real labeled data?


### Proposed method
Start from a strong self-supervised ASR backbone (e.g., a multilingual wav2vec-style encoder with a CTC/Transducer head) and keep it largely frozen. Insert lightweight, domain- and language-specific adapters in several transformer blocks, and add a single global, learnable acoustic prompt in the front end (a small time–frequency convolutional residual that perturbs inputs). Train only the adapters, the prompt, and the final prediction head on a few hours of real transcribed audio to anchor the mapping for the target language/domain. Maintain a small held-out dev set for thresholding and early stopping.

Create abundant paired data by synthesizing speech from large in-domain text. Use multi-speaker TTS when available; otherwise rely on cross-lingual TTS/voice conversion or a phoneme-based TTS seeded by grapheme-to-phoneme rules shared with related languages. Match target acoustics via augmentation and style transfer (reverberation, noise, channel, speaking rate, accent perturbations). Jointly train with two auxiliary objectives that do not need transcripts: masked acoustic modeling on unlabeled audio to stabilize the frozen backbone’s features, and a mix-and-separate branch trained on synthetic source mixtures to learn to suppress interferers and noise. Train or adapt a small domain language model on the same text for shallow fusion at decode time.

Leverage unlabeled in-domain audio with teacher-guided self-training. Generate pseudo-transcripts using a strong external teacher ASR and score each utterance and token for confidence. Equip the student with a cheap deterministic uncertainty head that reads pre-logit representations and outputs per-utterance confidence; filter or down-weight pseudo-labels using the minimum of teacher and student confidence. Aggregate data iteratively in a DAgger-like loop: each round, add high-confidence pseudo-labeled segments and a budget of disagreement-focused segments where the teacher is confident but the student errs, so learning targets the student’s blind spots. Balance batches across gold, synthetic, and pseudo data, gradually shifting weight toward confident in-domain pseudo data as training progresses.

Reduce domain mismatch with feature-level adversarial alignment between target-domain unlabeled audio and the synthetic/limited labeled pool, applied on the shared encoder features while keeping the backbone frozen. For low-confidence segments, replace hard labels with consistency regularization: enforce prediction stability under strong acoustic augmentations rather than trusting noisy text. Update the global acoustic prompt with an uncertainty-guided EMA so it adapts more on hard, uncertain samples and stabilizes on easy ones. To prevent confirmation bias, keep the external teacher fixed (or use a slow EMA teacher distinct from the student), gate all threshold updates on the held-out dev set, and stop self-training rounds when dev WER plateaus.

At inference, enable the learned acoustic prompt and target adapters, decode with beam search and shallow-fused domain LM, and optionally run the separation-enhancement branch for noisy inputs. This configuration preserves the pretrained model’s generalization, adds only small domain-specific parameters, and exploits synthetic text and unlabeled audio to approach competitive accuracy with minimal real transcripts.

### Rationale
The backbone contributes powerful acoustic-linguistic representations; adapting only small adapters and a global acoustic prompt exploits that capacity with few labeled examples, reflecting evidence that much task ability is already present in pretrained networks. Synthetic TTS pairs inject large, domain-relevant supervision while auxiliary masked modeling and mix-and-separate training improve robustness to noise and overlapping speech without extra labels. A teacher-driven, confidence-weighted self-training loop converts unlabeled audio into useful supervision and focuses learning where the student most needs it, while deterministic uncertainty filtering and dev-set gating curb self-training bias. Adversarial alignment and uncertainty-guided prompt updates further close the domain gap with minimal additional parameters.

Together, these pieces turn abundant unlabeled audio and text into effective training signals, keep the finetuning footprint small, and mitigate the failure modes of naive pseudo-labeling. The result is a practical path to competitive ASR accuracy in low-resource languages or specialized domains using only a few hours of real transcriptions.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

8. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

9. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)
   problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
   method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

10. [RL] Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters (1909.02119)
   problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
   method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

11. [MULTIMODAL] Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning (2412.02071)
   problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
   method:  Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

12. [ROBOTICS] Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting (2103.09555)
   problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.

13. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

21. [LLM_NLP] MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation (2509.21978)
   problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.

When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
