messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Speech recognition for low-resource languages or specialized
domains lacks transcribed audio; how can we leverage synthetic
data or transfer learning to reach competitive accuracy with
minimal real labeled data?


### Proposed method
Start from a strong self-supervised ASR backbone (e.g., a multilingual wav2vec-style encoder with a CTC/Transducer head) and keep it largely frozen. Insert lightweight, domain- and language-specific adapters in several transformer blocks, and add a single global, learnable acoustic prompt in the front end (a small time–frequency convolutional residual that perturbs inputs). Train only the adapters, the prompt, and the final prediction head on a few hours of real transcribed audio to anchor the mapping for the target language/domain. Maintain a small held-out dev set for thresholding and early stopping.

Create abundant paired data by synthesizing speech from large in-domain text. Use multi-speaker TTS when available; otherwise rely on cross-lingual TTS/voice conversion or a phoneme-based TTS seeded by grapheme-to-phoneme rules shared with related languages. Match target acoustics via augmentation and style transfer (reverberation, noise, channel, speaking rate, accent perturbations). Jointly train with two auxiliary objectives that do not need transcripts: masked acoustic modeling on unlabeled audio to stabilize the frozen backbone’s features, and a mix-and-separate branch trained on synthetic source mixtures to learn to suppress interferers and noise. Train or adapt a small domain language model on the same text for shallow fusion at decode time.

Leverage unlabeled in-domain audio with teacher-guided self-training. Generate pseudo-transcripts using a strong external teacher ASR and score each utterance and token for confidence. Equip the student with a cheap deterministic uncertainty head that reads pre-logit representations and outputs per-utterance confidence; filter or down-weight pseudo-labels using the minimum of teacher and student confidence. Aggregate data iteratively in a DAgger-like loop: each round, add high-confidence pseudo-labeled segments and a budget of disagreement-focused segments where the teacher is confident but the student errs, so learning targets the student’s blind spots. Balance batches across gold, synthetic, and pseudo data, gradually shifting weight toward confident in-domain pseudo data as training progresses.

Reduce domain mismatch with feature-level adversarial alignment between target-domain unlabeled audio and the synthetic/limited labeled pool, applied on the shared encoder features while keeping the backbone frozen. For low-confidence segments, replace hard labels with consistency regularization: enforce prediction stability under strong acoustic augmentations rather than trusting noisy text. Update the global acoustic prompt with an uncertainty-guided EMA so it adapts more on hard, uncertain samples and stabilizes on easy ones. To prevent confirmation bias, keep the external teacher fixed (or use a slow EMA teacher distinct from the student), gate all threshold updates on the held-out dev set, and stop self-training rounds when dev WER plateaus.

At inference, enable the learned acoustic prompt and target adapters, decode with beam search and shallow-fused domain LM, and optionally run the separation-enhancement branch for noisy inputs. This configuration preserves the pretrained model’s generalization, adds only small domain-specific parameters, and exploits synthetic text and unlabeled audio to approach competitive accuracy with minimal real transcripts.

### Rationale
The backbone contributes powerful acoustic-linguistic representations; adapting only small adapters and a global acoustic prompt exploits that capacity with few labeled examples, reflecting evidence that much task ability is already present in pretrained networks. Synthetic TTS pairs inject large, domain-relevant supervision while auxiliary masked modeling and mix-and-separate training improve robustness to noise and overlapping speech without extra labels. A teacher-driven, confidence-weighted self-training loop converts unlabeled audio into useful supervision and focuses learning where the student most needs it, while deterministic uncertainty filtering and dev-set gating curb self-training bias. Adversarial alignment and uncertainty-guided prompt updates further close the domain gap with minimal additional parameters.

Together, these pieces turn abundant unlabeled audio and text into effective training signals, keep the finetuning footprint small, and mitigate the failure modes of naive pseudo-labeling. The result is a practical path to competitive ASR accuracy in low-resource languages or specialized domains using only a few hours of real transcriptions.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

8. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

9. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)
   problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
   method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

10. [RL] Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters (1909.02119)
   problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
   method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

11. [MULTIMODAL] Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning (2412.02071)
   problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
   method:  Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

12. [ROBOTICS] Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting (2103.09555)
   problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.

13. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

Unlike static concatenation or fixed-ratio fusion strategies, DFU performs context-aware gating and multiplicative interaction, guided by local acoustic context and confidence estimates. 3. Filter and evolve: progressive pseudo label refining for semi-supervised automatic speech recognition (arXiv.org 2022, cites: 3)
   Fine-tuning self-supervised pre-trained models using pseudo-labels can effectively improve speech recognition performance. But, low-quality pseudo-labels can misguide decision boundaries and degrade performance. We propose a simple yet effective strategy to ﬁlter low-quality pseudo-labels to al-leviate this problem. Speciﬁcally, pseudo-labels are produced over the entire training set and ﬁltered via average probability scores calculated from the model’s output. Subsequently, an optimal percentage of utterances with high probability scores are considered reliable training data with trustworthy labels. The model is iteratively updated to correct the unreliable pseudo-labels to minimize the effect of noisy labels. The process above is repeated until unreliable pseudo-labels have been adequately corrected. Extensive experiments on LibriSpeech show that these ﬁltered samples enable the reﬁned model to yield more correct predictions, leading to better ASR performances under various experimental settings.

5. Iterative pseudo-forced alignment by acoustic CTC loss for self-supervised ASR domain adaptation (IberSPEECH Conference 2022, cites: 7)
   High-quality data labeling from specific domains is costly and human time-consuming. In this work, we propose a self-supervised domain adaptation method, based upon an iterative pseudo-forced alignment algorithm. The produced alignments are employed to customize an end-to-end Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) and iteratively refined. The algorithm is fed with frame-wise character posteriors produced by a seed ASR, trained with out-of-domain data, and optimized throughout a Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss. The alignments are computed iteratively upon a corpus of broadcast TV. The process is repeated by reducing the quantity of text to be aligned or expanding the alignment window until finding the best possible audio-text alignment. The starting timestamps, or temporal anchors, are produced uniquely based on the confidence score of the last aligned utterance. This score is computed with the paths of the CTC-alignment matrix. With this methodology, no human-revised text references are required. The obtained results, on both the Spanish RTVE2022 and CommonVoice databases, underpin the feasibility of using CTC-based systems to perform: highly accurate audio-text alignments, domain adaptation and semi-supervised training of end-to-end ASR.

6. Continuous Pseudo-Labeling from the Start (International Conference on Learning Representations 2022, cites: 17)
   Self-training (ST), or pseudo-labeling has sparked significant interest in the automatic speech recognition (ASR) community recently because of its success in harnessing unlabeled data. Unlike prior semi-supervised learning approaches that relied on iteratively regenerating pseudo-labels (PLs) from a trained model and using them to train a new model, recent state-of-the-art methods perform `continuous training' where PLs are generated using a very recent version of the model being trained. Nevertheless, these approaches still rely on bootstrapping the ST using an initial supervised learning phase where the model is trained on labeled data alone. We believe this has the potential for over-fitting to the labeled dataset in low resource settings and that ST from the start of training should reduce over-fitting. In this paper we show how we can do this by dynamically controlling the evolution of PLs during the training process in ASR. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study that shows the feasibility of generating PLs from the very start of the training. We are able to achieve this using two techniques that avoid instabilities which lead to degenerate models that do not generalize. Firstly, we control the evolution of PLs through a curriculum that uses the online changes in PLs to control the membership of the cache of PLs and improve generalization. Secondly, we find that by sampling transcriptions from the predictive distribution, rather than only using the best transcription, we can stabilize training further. With these techniques, our ST models match prior works without an external language model.

2. mDALU: Multi-Source Domain Adaptation and Label Unification with Partial Datasets (IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision 2020, cites: 27)
   One challenge of object recognition is to generalize to new domains, to more classes and/or to new modalities. This necessitates methods to combine and reuse existing datasets that may belong to different domains, have partial annotations, and/or have different data modalities. Our method consists of a partially-supervised adaptation stage and a fully-supervised adaptation stage. 1. Mix-fine-tune: An Alternate Fine-tuning Strategy for Domain Adaptation and Generalization of Low-resource ASR (ACM Multimedia Asia 2024, cites: 3)
   Self-supervised Learning (SSL) using extensive unlabeled speech data has significantly improved the performance of ASR models on datasets like LibriSpeech. However, few studies have addressed the issue of domain mismatch between the data used to pre-train and fine-tune ASR models. Moreover, the Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) principle, commonly used to train deep learning models, often causes the trained models to exhibit undesirable behaviors such as memorizing training data and being sensitive to adversarial examples. Thus, in this paper, we propose an alternate fine-tuning strategy, called Mix-fine-tune, to address domain mismatch in ASR systems and the limitations of the ERM training principle. Mix-fine-tune use a data-driven weighted sum of two speech sequences as input and the corresponding text sequences are used to calculate a weighted audio-text alignment Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss for fine-tuning a pre-trained model. Additionally, Mix-fine-tune incorporates the masked Contrastive Predictive Coding (CPC) loss, previously used exclusively for pre-training, into the fine-tuning process. Our novel strategy alternates between minimizing the CTC loss and the CPC loss to address the domain mismatch between pre-training and fine-tuning. We validate our method by fine-tuning different sizes of the Wav2Vec model using the public Air Traffic Control (ATC) corpus. The experiments show that Mix-fine-tune efficiently adapts the models pre-trained on general speech corpora like LibriSpeech to a specific domain (e.g., the air traffic control domain) by fine-turning.

However, collecting pixel-level annotations at scale is costly, motivating the use of unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) to transfer knowledge from annotated (source) to unlabeled (target) datasets. Existing UDA methods, primarily designed for generic visual tasks, struggle with the unique domain shifts in agricultural imagery caused by variations in crop morphology, growth stage, illumination, and imaging conditions. These challenges often result in oversegmentation, under segmentation, and crop-weed misclassification. To address these issues, we propose cross-crop UDA (CCUDA), a transformer-based UDA framework tailored for agricultural domain adaptation. CCUDA leverages mid-level cues—such as row patterns and background textures—that remain consistent across crop types and environments. It employs a hybrid training strategy that combines supervised learning on the source domain with self-training on the target domain through pseudolabels. To mitigate noise in pseudolabels, we introduce masked adversarial alignment, which focusses alignment on uncertain, low-confidence regions while preserving stable representations of confident pixels. Furthermore, to resolve the feature overlap in logit space between crops and weeds due to high interclass similarity, CCUDA incorporates a logit-distance regularizer that enforces explicit separation in logit space, enhancing class discriminability. Finally, a stage-aware sampling strategy balances data exposure across varying crop growth stages, addressing dataset imbalance. CCUDA integrates seamlessly with existing UDA frameworks and, when applied to four state-of-the-art baselines across eight agricultural datasets, achieves up to +5.23% higher mean intersection over union (mIoU) and a +10.04% improvement in crop-class IoU, establishing new benchmarks for robust and generalizable agricultural segmentation.

4. On the Relevance of Phoneme Duration Variability of Synthesized Training Data for Automatic Speech Recognition (Automatic Speech Recognition & Understanding 2023, cites: 5)
   Synthetic data generated by text-to-speech (TTS) systems can be used to improve automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems in low-resource or domain mismatch tasks. It has been shown that TTS-generated outputs still do not have the same qualities as real data. In this work we focus on the temporal structure of synthetic data and its relation to ASR training. By using a novel oracle setup we show how much the degradation of synthetic data quality is influenced by duration modeling in non-autoregressive (NAR) TTS. To get reference phoneme durations we use two common alignment methods, a hidden Markov Gaussian-mixture model (HMM-GMM) aligner and a neural connectionist temporal classification (CTC) aligner. Using a simple algorithm based on random walks we shift phoneme duration distributions of the TTS system closer to real durations, resulting in an improvement of an ASR system using synthetic data in a semi-supervised setting.

5. Doing More with Less: Data Augmentation for Sudanese Dialect Automatic Speech Recognition (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Although many Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems have been developed for Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) and Dialectal Arabic (DA), few studies have focused on dialect-specific implementations, particularly for low-resource Arabic dialects such as Sudanese. This paper presents a comprehensive study of data augmentation techniques for fine-tuning OpenAI Whisper models and establishes the first benchmark for the Sudanese dialect. Two augmentation strategies are investigated: (1) self-training with pseudo-labels generated from unlabeled speech, and (2) TTS-based augmentation using synthetic speech from the Klaam TTS system. The best-performing model, Whisper-Medium fine-tuned with combined self-training and TTS augmentation (28.4 hours), achieves a Word Error Rate (WER) of 57.1% on the evaluation set and 51.6% on an out-of-domain holdout set substantially outperforming zero-shot multilingual Whisper (78.8% WER) and MSA-specialized Arabic models (73.8-123% WER). All experiments used low-cost resources (Kaggle free tier and Lightning.ai trial), demonstrating that strategic data augmentation can overcome resource limitations for low-resource dialects and provide a practical roadmap for developing ASR systems for low-resource Arabic dialects and other marginalized language varieties. The models, evaluation benchmarks, and reproducible training pipelines are publicly released to facilitate future research on low-resource Arabic ASR.

6. Generalizing Large Language Model Usability Across Resource-Constrained (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success across a wide range of natural language tasks, and recent efforts have sought to extend their capabilities to multimodal domains and resource-constrained environments. However, existing approaches often rely on costly supervised fine-tuning or assume fixed training conditions, limiting their generalization when facing unseen modalities, limited data, or restricted compute resources. This dissertation presents a systematic study toward generalizing LLM usability under real-world constraints. First, it introduces a robust text-centric alignment framework that enables LLMs to seamlessly integrate diverse modalities-including text, images, tables, and any modalities - via natural language interfaces. This approach supports in-context adaptation to unseen or dynamically changing modalities without requiring retraining. To enhance robustness against noisy and missing modalities, an adversarial prompting technique is proposed, generating semantically challenging perturbations at the prompt level to stress-test model reliability. Beyond multimodal setting, the dissertation investigates inference-time optimization strategies for LLMs, leveraging prompt search and uncertainty quantification to improve performance without additional model training. This perspective offers an efficient alternative to scaling model parameters or retraining from scratch. Additionally, the work addresses low-resource domains such as Verilog code generation by designing correct-by-construction synthetic data pipelines and logic-enhanced reasoning models, achieving state-of-the-art performance with minimal data. Together, these contributions form a unified effort to enhance the adaptability, scalability, and efficiency of large language models under practical constraints.

Experiments using all labeled data of Librispeech achieve 1.8/3.3 WER on the clean/other test sets. When lowering the amount of labeled data to one hour, wav2vec 2.0 outperforms the previous state of the art on the 100 hour subset while using 100 times less labeled data. Using just ten minutes of labeled data and pre-training on 53k hours of unlabeled data still achieves 4.8/8.2 WER. This demonstrates the feasibility of speech recognition with limited amounts of labeled data.

2. Front-End Adapter: Adapting Front-End Input of Speech Based Self-Supervised Learning for Speech Recognition (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing 2023, cites: 4)
   Recent years have witnessed a boom in self-supervised learning (SSL) in various areas including speech processing. Speech based SSL models present promising performance in a range of speech related tasks. However, the training of SSL models is computationally expensive and a common practice is to fine-tune a released SSL model on the specific task. It is essential to use consistent front-end input during pre-training and fine-tuning. This consistency may introduce potential issues when the optimal front-end is not the same as that used in pre-training. In this paper, we propose a simple but effective front-end adapter to address this front-end discrepancy. By minimizing the distance between the outputs of different front-ends, the filterbank feature (Fbank) can be compatible with SSL models which are pre-trained with waveform. The experiment results demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed front-end adapter on several popular SSL models for the speech recognition task.

3. INTapt: Information-Theoretic Adversarial Prompt Tuning for Enhanced Non-Native Speech Recognition (Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics 2023, cites: 4)
   Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems have attained unprecedented performance with large speech models pre-trained based on self-supervised speech representation learning. However, these pre-trained speech models suffer from representational bias as they tend to better represent those prominent accents (i.e., native (L1) English accent) in the pre-training speech corpus than less represented accents, resulting in a deteriorated performance for non-native (L2) English accents. Although there have been some approaches to mitigate this issue, all of these methods require updating the pre-trained model weights. In this paper, we propose Information Theoretic Adversarial Prompt Tuning (INTapt), which introduces prompts concatenated to the original input that can re-modulate the attention of the pre-trained model such that the corresponding input resembles a native (L1) English speech without updating the backbone weights. INTapt is trained simultaneously in the following two manners: (1) adversarial training to reduce accent feature dependence between the original input and the prompt-concatenated input and (2) training to minimize CTC loss for improving ASR performance to a prompt-concatenated input. Experimental results show that INTapt improves the performance of L2 English and increases feature similarity between L2 and L1 accents.

4. Efficient Adapter Transfer of Self-Supervised Speech Models for Automatic Speech Recognition (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing 2022, cites: 82)
   Self-supervised learning (SSL) is a powerful tool that allows learning of underlying representations from unlabeled data. Transformer based models such as wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT are leading the field in the speech domain. Generally these models are fine-tuned on a small amount of labeled data for a downstream task such as Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR). This involves re-training the majority of the model for each task. Adapters are small lightweight modules which are commonly used in Natural Language Processing (NLP) to adapt pre-trained models to new tasks. In this paper we propose applying adapters to wav2vec 2.0 to reduce the number of parameters required for downstream ASR tasks, and increase scalability of the model to multiple tasks or languages. Using adapters we can perform ASR while training fewer than 10% of parameters per task compared to full fine-tuning with little degradation of performance. Ablations show that applying adapters into just the top few layers of the pre-trained network gives similar performance to full transfer, supporting the theory that higher pre-trained layers encode more phonemic information, and further optimizing efficiency.

5. Wav2vec-S: Semi-Supervised Pre-Training for Speech Recognition (arXiv.org 2021, cites: 3)
   <no abstract>

6. Parameter-Efficient Federal-Tuning Enhances Privacy Preserving for Speech Emotion Recognition (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing 2025, cites: 0)
   The Pre-trained Speech Models (PSMs) generate universal speech representations using self-supervised or weakly-supervised learning from large-scale datasets. It achieves promising performance when fine-tuned for specific tasks such as Speech Emotion Recognition (SER). However, fine-tuning on various datasets requires storing the entire model’s weight parameters, complicating real-world deployment. Additionally, centralized fine-tuning relies on user data, posing significant privacy risks. To address these challenges, we propose employing Federated Learning (FL) for fine-tuning PSMs with a Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) method. By embedding trainable layers in the feed-forward layers of the pre-trained model, we keep the backbone model frozen and only update the trainable layer parameters during federated training, significantly reducing parameter transmission. Specifically, we evaluated the performance of downstream model fine-tuning, adapter tuning, embedding prompt tuning, and LoRA within a federated fine-tuning framework for PSMs, demonstrating the framework’s feasibility and effectiveness. Furthermore, attribute inference attack tests showed that gender inference results on three datasets were at chance levels.

7. An Adapter Based Pre-Training for Efficient and Scalable Self-Supervised Speech Representation Learning (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing 2021, cites: 31)
   We present a method for transferring pre-trained self-supervised (SSL) speech representations to multiple languages. There is an abundance of unannotated speech, so creating self-supervised representations from raw audio and fine-tuning on small annotated datasets is a promising direction to build speech recognition systems. SSL models generally perform SSL on raw audio in a pre-training phase and then fine-tune on a small fraction of annotated data. Such models have produced state of the art results for ASR. However, these models are very expensive to pre-train. We use an existing wav2vec 2.0 model and tackle the problem of learning new language representations while utilizing existing model knowledge. Crucially we do so without catastrophic forgetting of the existing language representation. We use adapter modules to speed up pre-training a new language task. Our model can decrease pre-training times by 32% when learning a new language task, and learn this new audio-language representation without forgetting previous language representation. We evaluate by applying these language representations to automatic speech recognition.

8. Improving Low-Resource Speech Recognition with Pretrained Speech Models: Continued Pretraining vs. Semi-Supervised Training (arXiv.org 2022, cites: 10)
   Self-supervised Transformer based models, such as wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT, have produced significant improvements over existing approaches to automatic speech recognition (ASR). This is evident in the performance of the wav2vec 2.0 based pretrained XLSR-53 model across many languages when fine-tuned with available labeled data. However, the performance from finetuning these models can be dependent on the amount of in-language or similar-to-in-language data included in the pretraining dataset. In this paper we investigate continued pretraining (CoPT) with unlabeled in-language audio data on the XLSR-53 pretrained model in several low-resource languages. CoPT is more computationally efficient than semi-supervised training (SST), the standard approach of utilizing unlabeled data in ASR, since it omits the need for pseudo-labeling of the unlabeled data. We show CoPT results in word error rates (WERs), equal to or slightly better than using SST. In addition, we show that using the CoPT model for pseudo-labeling, and using these labels in SST, results in further improvements in WER.

9. An Experimental Comparison between Low-Resource Semi-Supervised and High-Resource Supervised Automatic Speech Recognition Models (IEEE International Conference on Multimedia and Expo 2022, cites: 1)
   Automatic speech recognition (ASR) is an important module in many multimedia applications. Recently, semi-supervised ASR has attracted increasing attention, which can be classified into self-supervised learning and self-training. It was shown that the combination of the two methods is beneficial for ASR on open datasets, e.g., LibriSpeech. However, it is still not completely clear how the semi-supervised model behaves on more challenging industrial datasets compared with supervised approaches using high-resource labeled data. In this paper, we therefore present an experimental study on the combination of self-supervised learning (e.g., wav2vec 2.0) and self-training (e.g., noisy student) in a low-resource industrial setting. The in-domain pre-trained and fine-tuned wav2vec 2.0 model is utilized to teach a baseline VGG-Transformer by pseudo-labeling in an industrial setting. Results reveal that without extra language models the combined semi-supervised acoustic model using much less labeled data can perform as well as the supervised counterpart, which would be rather beneficial for the application of semi-supervised ASR models in low-resource scenarios.

10. UniPET-SPK: A Unified Framework for Parameter-Efficient Tuning of Pre-Trained Speech Models for Robust Speaker Verification (IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech, and Language Processing 2025, cites: 3)
   With excellent generalization ability, self-supervised speech models have shown impressive performance on various downstream speech tasks in the pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm. However, as the size of pre-trained models grows, fine-tuning becomes practically unfeasible due to expanding computation and storage requirements, as well as the risk of overfitting. In this study, we concentrate on exploring parameter-efficient tuning (PET) methods for adapting large-scale pre-trained self-supervised speech models to the speaker verification task. Correspondingly, we propose three parameter-efficient tuning methods: i) an adapter-tuning method, ii) a prompt-tuning method, and iii) a unified framework that effectively incorporates adapter-tuning and prompt-tuning with a dynamically learnable gating mechanism. First, we propose the Inner+Inter Adapter framework, which inserts two types of adapters into pre-trained models, allowing for adaptation of latent features within the intermediate Transformer layers and output embeddings from all Transformer layers, through a parallel adapter design. Second, we propose the Deep Speaker Prompting method that concatenates trainable prompt tokens into the input space of pre-trained models to guide adaptation. Lastly, we propose the UniPET-SPK, a unified framework that effectively incorporates these two alternate PET methods into a single framework with a dynamic trainable gating mechanism. The proposed UniPET-SPK learns to find the optimal mixture of PET methods to match different datasets and scenarios. We conduct a comprehensive set of experiments on several datasets to validate the effectiveness of the proposed parameter-efficient tuning methods. Experimental results on the VoxCeleb, CN-Celeb, and the 1$^{\mathrm{{st}}}$48-UTD forensic datasets demonstrate that the proposed UniPET-SPK can consistently outperform the two PET methods, fine-tuning, and other parameter-efficient tuning methods, achieving superior performance while updating only 5.4% of the parameters. We further conduct experiments on the CN-Celeb and 1$^{\mathrm{{st}}}$48-UTD datasets to demonstrate the robustness and generalization ability of the proposed methods for speaker verification in different languages and more challenging scenarios.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Speech recognition for low-resource languages or specialized
domains lacks transcribed audio; how can we leverage synthetic
data or transfer learning to reach competitive accuracy with
minimal real labeled data?


### Proposed method
Start from a strong self-supervised ASR backbone (e.g., a multilingual wav2vec-style encoder with a CTC/Transducer head) and keep it largely frozen. Insert lightweight, domain- and language-specific adapters in several transformer blocks, and add a single global, learnable acoustic prompt in the front end (a small time–frequency convolutional residual that perturbs inputs). Train only the adapters, the prompt, and the final prediction head on a few hours of real transcribed audio to anchor the mapping for the target language/domain. Maintain a small held-out dev set for thresholding and early stopping.

Create abundant paired data by synthesizing speech from large in-domain text. Use multi-speaker TTS when available; otherwise rely on cross-lingual TTS/voice conversion or a phoneme-based TTS seeded by grapheme-to-phoneme rules shared with related languages. Match target acoustics via augmentation and style transfer (reverberation, noise, channel, speaking rate, accent perturbations). Jointly train with two auxiliary objectives that do not need transcripts: masked acoustic modeling on unlabeled audio to stabilize the frozen backbone’s features, and a mix-and-separate branch trained on synthetic source mixtures to learn to suppress interferers and noise. Train or adapt a small domain language model on the same text for shallow fusion at decode time.

Leverage unlabeled in-domain audio with teacher-guided self-training. Generate pseudo-transcripts using a strong external teacher ASR and score each utterance and token for confidence. Equip the student with a cheap deterministic uncertainty head that reads pre-logit representations and outputs per-utterance confidence; filter or down-weight pseudo-labels using the minimum of teacher and student confidence. Aggregate data iteratively in a DAgger-like loop: each round, add high-confidence pseudo-labeled segments and a budget of disagreement-focused segments where the teacher is confident but the student errs, so learning targets the student’s blind spots. Balance batches across gold, synthetic, and pseudo data, gradually shifting weight toward confident in-domain pseudo data as training progresses.

Reduce domain mismatch with feature-level adversarial alignment between target-domain unlabeled audio and the synthetic/limited labeled pool, applied on the shared encoder features while keeping the backbone frozen. For low-confidence segments, replace hard labels with consistency regularization: enforce prediction stability under strong acoustic augmentations rather than trusting noisy text. Update the global acoustic prompt with an uncertainty-guided EMA so it adapts more on hard, uncertain samples and stabilizes on easy ones. To prevent confirmation bias, keep the external teacher fixed (or use a slow EMA teacher distinct from the student), gate all threshold updates on the held-out dev set, and stop self-training rounds when dev WER plateaus.

At inference, enable the learned acoustic prompt and target adapters, decode with beam search and shallow-fused domain LM, and optionally run the separation-enhancement branch for noisy inputs. This configuration preserves the pretrained model’s generalization, adds only small domain-specific parameters, and exploits synthetic text and unlabeled audio to approach competitive accuracy with minimal real transcripts.

### Rationale
The backbone contributes powerful acoustic-linguistic representations; adapting only small adapters and a global acoustic prompt exploits that capacity with few labeled examples, reflecting evidence that much task ability is already present in pretrained networks. Synthetic TTS pairs inject large, domain-relevant supervision while auxiliary masked modeling and mix-and-separate training improve robustness to noise and overlapping speech without extra labels. A teacher-driven, confidence-weighted self-training loop converts unlabeled audio into useful supervision and focuses learning where the student most needs it, while deterministic uncertainty filtering and dev-set gating curb self-training bias. Adversarial alignment and uncertainty-guided prompt updates further close the domain gap with minimal additional parameters.

Together, these pieces turn abundant unlabeled audio and text into effective training signals, keep the finetuning footprint small, and mitigate the failure modes of naive pseudo-labeling. The result is a practical path to competitive ASR accuracy in low-resource languages or specialized domains using only a few hours of real transcriptions.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.

For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.
